user_prompt: explain bernoulli’s principle using an airplane wing and airflow visualization

style_bible.visual_style: Realistic with clear, sharp details emphasizing aerodynamic shapes and airflow visualization using colored smoke or digital flow lines
style_bible.camera_rules: Use smooth, continuous camera movements with gradual zooms and pans to maintain spatial orientation and avoid jump cuts
style_bible.lighting_rules: Soft, natural daylight with directional lighting from above-left to enhance contours and flow visualization, consistent across scenes
style_bible.continuity_rules: Maintain the airplane wing as the primary subject in a consistent sky environment with subtle cloud background; airflow visualization style remains uniform

scenes.prompt:
SCENE INTENT: Introduce a stationary airplane wing with airflow beginning to move around it.

GLOBAL CONTINUITY:
- primary subject: a medium-sized airplane wing, smooth metallic surface, 3 meters span
- environment: clear blue sky with light wispy clouds, calm weather

SHOT 1 (0–3s):
- framing: medium shot, wing centered horizontally
- lens: 35mm
- camera movement: slow dolly forward
- subject & action: wing is stationary, airflow lines start appearing at leading edge
- environment details: light clouds, blue sky gradient, subtle horizon line
- lighting: soft directional light from top-left, neutral color temp
- cinematic look: clean film stock, moderate contrast, shallow depth of field focusing on wing leading edge

SHOT 2 (3–7s):
- framing: medium-close, wing leading edge left of frame
- lens: 50mm
- camera movement: slow pan right following airflow
- subject & action: colored airflow lines (blue and red) begin to flow smoothly over and under the wing
- environment details: clouds slightly blurred due to camera movement, sky remains bright
- lighting: consistent soft directional lighting
- cinematic look: natural colors, slight vignette to focus on airflow

SHOT 3 (7–10s):
- framing: close-up on wing upper surface
- lens: 85mm
- camera movement: static
- subject & action: airflow lines clearly separate above and below wing, showing faster flow on top
- environment details: wing texture visible, sky softly blurred
- lighting: directional light highlights wing curvature
- cinematic look: sharp focus on airflow lines, subtle film grain

AVOID: text, logos, subtitles, watermarks, extra limbs, distorted faces, flicker, glitch
scenes.negative_prompt: text, logos, subtitles, watermarks, extra limbs, distorted faces, flicker, glitch
scenes.camera: slow dolly forward, then slow pan right, ending static close-up
scenes.audio_cue: Narrator introduces Bernoulli’s principle and explains the airplane wing's role in generating lift by airflow.

scenes.prompt:
SCENE INTENT: Show airflow accelerating over the wing and pressure differences forming.

GLOBAL CONTINUITY:
- primary subject: same airplane wing, metallic surface, 3 meters span
- environment: clear blue sky with light wispy clouds, calm weather

SHOT 1 (0–3s):
- framing: medium shot, wing angled slightly upward
- lens: 35mm
- camera movement: slow tilt upward
- subject & action: airflow lines intensify, blue lines above wing speed up and narrow
- environment details: clouds in background, sunlit sky
- lighting: soft directional light from top-left, warm color temp
- cinematic look: enhanced contrast to emphasize flow speed

SHOT 2 (3–7s):
- framing: medium-close, wing upper surface dominant
- lens: 50mm
- camera movement: slow dolly right
- subject & action: visible pressure zones appear as subtle color gradients (blue low pressure above, red higher pressure below)
- environment details: blurred clouds, bright sky
- lighting: consistent soft lighting
- cinematic look: natural colors with slight saturation boost

SHOT 3 (7–10s):
- framing: close-up on wing trailing edge
- lens: 85mm
- camera movement: slow pan left
- subject & action: airflow lines converge smoothly behind wing, showing lift generation
- environment details: wing texture sharp, sky softly blurred
- lighting: directional light highlights trailing edge
- cinematic look: crisp focus with shallow depth of field

AVOID: text, logos, subtitles, watermarks, extra limbs, distorted faces, flicker, glitch
scenes.negative_prompt: text, logos, subtitles, watermarks, extra limbs, distorted faces, flicker, glitch
scenes.camera: slow tilt upward, slow dolly right, slow pan left
scenes.audio_cue: Narrator explains how airflow speed differences create pressure variations that generate lift on the wing.

scenes.prompt:
SCENE INTENT: Demonstrate the resulting lift force lifting the wing with airflow continuing smoothly.

GLOBAL CONTINUITY:
- primary subject: same airplane wing, metallic surface, 3 meters span
- environment: clear blue sky with light wispy clouds, calm weather

SHOT 1 (0–3s):
- framing: medium shot, wing slightly tilted upward
- lens: 35mm
- camera movement: slow crane up
- subject & action: wing begins to lift slowly, airflow lines stable and smooth
- environment details: sky with soft clouds, horizon faint
- lighting: soft directional light from top-left, neutral color temp
- cinematic look: balanced contrast, natural colors

SHOT 2 (3–7s):
- framing: medium-close, wing center
- lens: 50mm
- camera movement: slow dolly backward
- subject & action: wing rises steadily, airflow lines remain continuous and clear
- environment details: clouds in background, sky bright
- lighting: consistent soft lighting
- cinematic look: sharp focus on wing and flow lines

SHOT 3 (7–10s):
- framing: wide shot, wing fully lifted in frame
- lens: 24mm
- camera movement: slow pan left
- subject & action: wing held aloft by airflow, final clear frame showing lift effect
- environment details: expansive sky, soft clouds
- lighting: directional light enhances wing silhouette
- cinematic look: cinematic wide shot with natural colors

AVOID: text, logos, subtitles, watermarks, extra limbs, distorted faces, flicker, glitch
scenes.negative_prompt: text, logos, subtitles, watermarks, extra limbs, distorted faces, flicker, glitch
scenes.camera: slow crane up, slow dolly backward, slow pan left
scenes.audio_cue: Narrator concludes by describing how Bernoulli’s principle results in lift that allows the wing to rise.
